spec_reconstruction.domain: Current U.S. federal banking board obligations for Category I global systemically important bank holding companies and their insured depository institution subsidiaries.
spec_reconstruction.unit_of_analysis: Each discrete current, in-force board-level obligation, with source, duty, actor allocation, committee or director-type distinction, and reform relevance.
structural_axes: entity tier: Category I GSIB BHC versus insured depository institution subsidiary
actor: full board versus board committee
committee type: audit, risk, compensation, nominating/governance, fiduciary, compliance, technology, or other designated committee
director type: inside, outside, independent, non-independent, risk-expert, interested director, and disqualified director distinctions
source tier: statute, regulation, enforceable guideline, supervisory guidance, examination manual, enforcement template, and interagency standard
issuing authority: Federal Reserve, OCC, FDIC, CFPB, FFIEC/interagency, FinCEN/OFAC where relevant

regulatory domain: safety and soundness, capital, stress testing, liquidity, recovery, resolution, operational resilience, cybersecurity, third-party risk, model risk, BSA/AML, consumer compliance, incentive compensation, audit, internal controls, fiduciary, affiliate, and insider-transaction governance
actor_classes_required: Category I GSIB BHC full board
insured depository institution subsidiary full board
source_universe: Federal Reserve regulations and supervisory guidance
OCC regulations, enforceable guidelines, bulletins, and manuals
FDIC regulations, guidance, examination material, and IDI resolution-planning rules
CFPB supervision and examination materials relevant to consumer compliance governance
Federal statutes underlying board-level banking obligations
coverage_areas: consumer compliance, fair lending, UDAAP, privacy, and CRA oversight
audit, internal control, financial reporting, fiduciary, insider, affiliate, and director-qualification obligations
spec_reconstruction.audience_use: Bank directors, legal counsel, and policymakers seeking a working reform document to identify duplicative, overlapping, and burdensome board-level requirements.
spec_reconstruction.form_depth_expectations: Granular, citation-specific 40-60 page inventory with current-law status, actor mapping, committee/director distinctions, and reform-useful sludge analysis.

material_omissions: The report is scoped to the broad 'U.S. banking sector' rather than Category I GSIB BHCs and their IDI subsidiaries.
It is a 10-row topical summary, not a citation-level inventory of discrete current obligations.
It lacks BHC-versus-IDI subsidiary mapping and does not separate Category I obligations from community-bank, public-company, national-bank, foreign, private, or general banking materials.
It does not systematically map duties to full board, audit committee, risk committee, compensation committee, nominating/governance committee, or director types.
Model risk governance, resolution planning, recovery planning, operational resilience, third-party risk management, and incentive compensation are missing or only indirectly mentioned.
It uses non-U.S. and non-federal materials, including Nigeria's CAMA 2020 and a Swiss-bank succession survey, in a U.S. federal authority inventory.
It includes proposed, withdrawn, nonbinding, private, and news/commentary materials as if they were current controlling authorities.
Several cited legal references are wrong, including FFIEC 312 for Call Reports, FDIC section 326 for audit committees, and Federal Reserve capital rules as 12 CFR Part 23.

results.citation: 1933 Banking Act / Section 5040 of the Federal Reserve 'Bank Rating Manual'
results.reason: The cited source does not support fiduciary duties as stated; Federal Reserve director duties are in supervision manual material such as 'Duties and Responsibilities of Directors,' not a 1933 Banking Act section 5040: https://www.federalreserve.gov/Boarddocs/SupManual/cbem/cbem_p5.pdf

results.citation: FDIC 'Statement Concerning the Responsibilities of Bank Directors and Officers'
results.reason: The FDIC statement supports general fiduciary care/loyalty concepts for bank directors, though it is not a granular obligation inventory: https://www.fdic.gov/regulations/laws/rules/responsibilities-bank-directors-officers.pdf

results.citation: OCC Guidelines risk-governance framework for national banks >= $50 billion
results.reason: OCC heightened standards in 12 CFR Part 30 Appendix D support a risk-governance framework and board active oversight duties for covered OCC institutions: https://www.occ.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2014/nr-occ-2014-117.html

results.citation: Basel Committee 'Corporate Governance Principles for Banks' as U.S. federal/Basel III principles
results.reason: The Basel document is international nonbinding guidance, not a U.S. federal authority imposing current board obligations: https://www.bis.org/bcbs/publ/d328.htm

results.citation: AABD 'Bank Director Regulatory Burden Report' identifying >800 provisions
results.reason: AABD materials support the private association's quantitative burden claim, but they are not primary legal authority: https://aabd.org/regulatory-relief-dont-forget-bank-directors/

results.citation: AABD/BuckleySandler webinar claim that boards approve more than 50 written policies annually
results.reason: AABD EGRPRA materials support an over-fifty written-policy burden claim, but it is not a primary citation to specific board obligations: https://aabd.org/regulatory-relief-dont-forget-bank-directors/

results.citation: Bank Secrecy Act / 31 CFR Part 1020
results.reason: FinCEN's bank AML program rule supports board approval of the AML program, but not the report's broader certification claims: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-31/subtitle-B/chapter-X/part-1020/subpart-B/section-1020.210

results.citation: CFPB consumer-protection rules (TILA, RESPA, ECOA, Fair Housing) requiring board certification of effective compliance
results.reason: The cited consumer statutes/rules impose institutional obligations and examiner expectations, but they do not support a general board certification duty.

results.citation: OCC fair-housing data-collection rule rescinded 2026 as board compliance-program authority
results.reason: OCC 2026 final rules support rescission of certain data-collection requirements, not a current board-level compliance obligation: https://www.occ.treas.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2026/nr-occ-2026-13.html

results.citation: FRB capital-adequacy regulations (Reg < 12 CFR Part 23)
results.reason: Federal Reserve capital rules are not 'Reg < 12 CFR Part 23'; the current capital rule is Regulation Q/12 CFR Part 217: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-II/subchapter-A/part-217

results.citation: FDICIA section 317 capital-adequacy reporting thresholds
results.reason: Searches did not support the cited section as the source for the stated capital-adequacy board reporting thresholds; the claim lacks a usable primary citation.

results.citation: Basel III implementation proposals and Bowman speech, March 2026
results.reason: Bowman's March 2026 speech supports a reform discussion about capital-rule simplification, but not current board-level obligations: https://www.federalreserve.gov/newsevents/speech/bowman20260312a.htm

results.citation: FDIC Guidelines requiring independent directors and a risk-committee chair
results.reason: The relevant FDIC corporate-governance proposal was withdrawn in 2025 and does not supply current in-force board obligations: https://www.fdic.gov/news/press-releases/2025/fdic-withdraws-proposed-corporate-governance-and-risk-management-guidelines

results.citation: Call-report (FFIEC 312) certifications
results.reason: Call Reports are FFIEC 031/041/051, not FFIEC 312; director attestation exists, but this citation is wrong: https://www.ffiec.gov/ffiec_report_forms.htm

results.citation: FDIC section 370 proposed board attestation for depositor-ownership IT testing
results.reason: Current Part 370 compliance certification is by the CEO or COO, not a current board attestation duty: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-III/subchapter-B/part-370/section-370.10

results.citation: OCC section 41 annual corporate-filings
results.reason: 12 CFR Part 41 is the OCC's Fair Credit Reporting regulation, not an annual corporate-filing board obligation: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-I/part-41

results.citation: FRB 'Management-Plan' requirements (FR B-1)
results.reason: Targeted searches did not identify a Federal Reserve 'FR B-1' management-plan authority supporting the stated strategic-plan approval duty.

results.citation: CAMA 2020 in Nigeria and AABD succession survey of Swiss banks
results.reason: These are not U.S. federal banking authorities and cannot support the requested current U.S. Category I GSIB board-obligation inventory.

results.citation: FDIC section 326 audit-committee charter
results.reason: FDIC Part 326 concerns BSA compliance; audit committee obligations are in FDIC Part 363: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-III/subchapter-B/part-326

results.citation: Executive Order 14331 (2021) and OCC/FDIC emerging ESG guidance (2025-2026)
results.reason: Executive Order 14331 is a 2025 debanking order, not a 2021 ESG authority; federal banking agencies also withdrew climate-risk principles in 2025: https://www.federalreserve.gov/newsevents/pressreleases/bcreg20251016a.htm

actor_mapping.note: The report uses broad phrases like 'typical board-level duty(s)' and does not map obligations to full board, specific required committees, entity tier, or director type.

binding_vs_guidance.note: The report blends statutes, regulations, proposals, withdrawn rules, private association reports, international guidance, state/non-U.S. material, and examiner practice as if they were comparable current authorities.

reform_usefulness.note: The report identifies useful sludge themes such as duplication, management-in-character duties, and attestation creep, but the reform value is undermined by incorrect sources and lack of obligation-level granularity.

critical_flags.evidence: The report cites "FRB's capital-adequacy regulations (Reg < 12 CFR Part 23)".
The report cites "FDIC § 326 (audit-committee charter)" even though Part 326 is BSA compliance.
The report says "FDIC Guidelines (independent directors, risk-committee chair)" as current authority.
The report says boards must "Certify that management's compliance program is effective".
The report says "both OCC and FDIC require majority independent directors and separate risk committees".
The title claims "Comprehensive inventory of board-of-directors obligations".
The report covers "the U.S. banking sector" and cites Nigeria and Swiss-bank materials.
The report claims it remains "anchored in the controlling U.S. federal authorities".

D1_coverage.evidence: The report is titled "Comprehensive inventory of board-of-directors obligations".
D1_coverage.rationale: The report touches several broad topical categories, but it is not a Category I GSIB BHC/IDI inventory, lacks required actor classes, and omits or barely treats major required domains. F6 caps the effective score.

D2_integrity.rationale: The citation list is dominated by non-primary, irrelevant, private, foreign, commentary, or wrong references. Multiple central citations are wrong-attributed, so F2/F7 cap the effective score.

D3_accuracy.evidence: The report says boards must "Certify that management's compliance program is effective".
D3_accuracy.rationale: The report repeatedly invents or overstates board obligations, treats withdrawn/proposed materials as current, and misidentifies basic regulatory sources. Central F3/F4 caps D3 at zero.

D4_sludge_analysis.rationale: It has some useful sludge vocabulary and practical themes, including duplication, attestation creep, and management-in-character burdens. The analysis remains superficial and not source-actionable.

D5_structure.evidence: The report uses a table headed "Typical statutory/regulatory source".
D5_structure.rationale: The topical table is readable, but it is too shallow, uses generic sources and duties, and appends an undifferentiated 100-item citation list rather than a working 40-60 page citation-level inventory.

D6_calibration.evidence: The report claims it is "anchored in the controlling U.S. federal authorities".
D6_calibration.rationale: The report substantially overclaims comprehensiveness and authority, does not flag uncertainty, and does not distinguish current binding law from guidance, proposals, foreign law, or private commentary. F5 caps D6 at one.

strengths: D5: The report attempts a compact topical table with source, board-duty, and sludge-comment columns.
D4: It identifies relevant sludge themes, including duplication, management-in-character duties, attestation creep, and resource disparity.
D2: A few cited sources are real and relevant, including the FDIC director-responsibility statement, OCC heightened standards, FinCEN AML program rule, and AABD burden materials.
weaknesses: D2/D3: Many central legal citations are wrong, including FFIEC 312 for Call Reports, FDIC section 326 for audit committees, and Federal Reserve capital rules as 12 CFR Part 23.
D1: The report materially misses the requested Category I GSIB BHC and IDI-subsidiary scope and instead surveys broad U.S., community-bank, public-company, international, and foreign materials.
D3: It invents or overstates board obligations, such as a general duty to certify management's compliance program and current FDIC majority-independent-director/risk-committee requirements.
D6: It falsely presents itself as comprehensive and controlling despite mixing binding law, guidance, proposals, withdrawn rules, private reports, news articles, and non-U.S. sources.
D5: It lacks a granular obligation inventory, citation-level pin cites, actor columns, current-law status, committee mapping, and director-type distinctions.
prioritized_remediation: Discard the current authority table and rebuild from primary sources only: eCFR, U.S. Code, Federal Reserve/OCC/FDIC/CFPB/FFIEC materials, and current agency guidance.
Create one row per discrete obligation with columns for entity tier, actor, committee, director type, source type, citation, duty, cadence, documentation, and reform lever.
Remove non-U.S., private, proposal-only, withdrawn, and commentary sources from the legal inventory; keep them only in a separate background appendix if useful.
Verify every specific citation, especially CFR part numbers, FFIEC form numbers, FDIC/OCC rule references, and executive-order years.

Add missing Category I GSIB domains, especially model risk, resolution planning, recovery/current resilience obligations, third-party risk, operational resilience, compensation, and committee-specific requirements.
narrative.reliance_recommendation: Do not rely on this report for legal, board, or policy reform work except as a rough list of themes to re-research from scratch.
